title: Beeswax leather conditioner
subtitle: A conditioning and protective paste for leather shoes, bags, and furniture
excerpt: A simple two-ingredient paste of beeswax pellets and sweet almond oil that conditions and waterproofs leather. Apply with a soft cloth, buff to a low sheen. Works on smooth leather shoes, boots, bags, and jackets.
sourceNotes: Beeswax as a leather protectant: traditional use, documented in British cordwaining traditions. Beeswax fills the micro-pores in the leather surface and creates a hydrophobic barrier. Sweet almond oil (high oleic acid) as a leather conditioner: penetrates the leather fibre matrix to prevent cracking. Traditional leather care recipes documented in leathercraft and shoemaking literature.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small tin (~50 g, sufficient for multiple applications on 2 to 3 pairs of shoes)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. For a softer cream-consistency conditioner, reduce the beeswax to 25% of total formula. For a firmer paste, increase to 45%.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 hours to set before using. The tin can be stored and used over many months.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store in the covered tin at room temperature. Keeps two years. Beeswax is stable; the oil may eventually oxidise but at a very slow rate given the large beeswax proportion. Discard if the paste develops a sharp rancid smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which beeswax fully liquefies. At room temperature the finished conditioner is a firm paste; when rubbed on leather, body heat from the hand softens the wax and allows it to penetrate the leather surface.

content.text: Leather needs two things to stay supple and water-resistant: a conditioning oil to keep the protein fibres from drying and cracking, and a wax to fill the surface pores and create a water-resistant barrier. This conditioner uses sweet almond oil as the conditioning carrier and beeswax as the protectant. The ratio here (35% beeswax to 65% oil by weight) produces a firm paste at room temperature that softens with the friction of application. At a higher beeswax percentage (45%+) the paste is harder and more waterproofing; at a lower percentage (25%) it is softer and more conditioning but gives less weather protection. The formula is fragrance-free because many leathers are sensitive to essential oils and fragrance compounds. It is a water-free
formula with a two-year shelf life.

items.prepNote: 35% of formula; yellow pellets for a slightly honey-tinted finish, white pellets for a neutral finish

content.text: Weigh the beeswax pellets into the top of the double boiler. Melt over gently simmering water until fully liquid. Add the sweet almond oil and stir to combine. Remove from heat and let the mixture cool to around 50°C before pouring into the tin; pouring too hot can cause the tin to deform or the wax to shrink unevenly.
Pour the warm mixture into the metal tin. Leave undisturbed for 2 hours until fully set. The surface should be smooth and firm. The
of the finished conditioner is above room temperature, so it will stay firm in a cool room but soften noticeably in direct sun or a hot car.

Clean the leather surface first with a damp cloth and let it dry fully. Dip a soft cloth or cotton pad into the tin to pick up a small amount of conditioner. Apply to the leather in circular motions; the friction of rubbing softens the wax and helps it penetrate. Use a small amount and build up with additional applications rather than applying too much at once. Leave for 5 minutes, then buff to a low sheen with a clean cloth. Test on a hidden area first with pale or light-coloured leather; some oils can darken light leathers slightly.
Suitable for smooth, full-grain leather shoes, boots, belts, bags, and jackets. Not suitable for suede, nubuck, or patent leather. May darken undyed or very pale leather; test on a hidden area first.
Store with the lid on in a cool place. The paste keeps two years. Discard if an off smell develops.
